does: This paper presents COBALT, a Z3 SMT-based formal verification system designed to detect arithmetic vulnerability patterns (CWE-190/191/195: integer overflow, underflow, and wrap-around) in C/C++ sandbox infrastructure before deployment. The core method encodes vulnerability-prone code patterns into satisfiability modulo theories problems, leveraging the Z3 solver to produce either concrete exploit witnesses (SAT verdicts) or formal guarantees of safety under specified bounds (UNSAT verdicts). The validated contributions demonstrate COBALT's effectiveness across four production codebases—NASA cFE, wolfSSL, Eclipse Mosquitto, and NASA F Prime—generating reproducible formal encodings and verified solver outputs that align with known security outcomes, establishing practical detection capability in real systems.
connects: This work responds directly to the April 2026 Mythos sandbox escape incident, which exposed that advanced model containment infrastructure remains vulnerable to formally characterizable arithmetic bugs despite significant focus on model alignment and safety. The paper builds on established SMT-based program verification techniques and the extensive literature on arithmetic overflow vulnerabilities in C/C++, particularly the CWE classification work identifying these patterns as pervasive in systems code. It extends prior formal methods approaches by specifically targeting the containment and sandbox infrastructure layer—the boundary between models and external systems—rather than model behavior itself, connecting to broader work on verifying security-critical systems before deployment and addressing gaps in pre-flight assurance for frontier model deployment pipelines.
enables: By establishing formal verification machinery for arithmetic vulnerabilities in infrastructure code, this work enables more rigorous pre-deployment certification of sandbox and containment systems for frontier models. It points toward integrated verification workflows where infrastructure supporting advanced AI systems receives formal security assurance comparable to safety-critical aerospace and embedded systems. The approach could extend to automated vulnerability detection in continuous deployment pipelines, potentially creating higher barriers to the kinds of subtle infrastructure bugs that enabled past escape incidents. Future work might generalize the SMT-encoding methodology to other vulnerability classes beyond arithmetic flaws, scale formal verification to larger codebases, and establish certification standards requiring formal verification for AI containment infrastructure.
